strict_win_deltas.delta: P1 adds both a Commands entry and a matching Examples entry ('bench-cli cart --input cart.json') for the new command, mirroring the existing hello/version pattern, while P2's hunk (@@ -10,6 +10,7 @@) only adds a Commands line and leaves the Examples section untouched, breaking the file's established parallel structure.

P2 wraps the entire cart case in `try { ... } catch (error) { writeCartError(error); }` with a fallback to `{ error: 'cart_error' }` for any non-cartFail exception, guaranteeing exit 2 and a single JSON stderr object even on an unanticipated crash, whereas P1's runCart has no top-level catch so an unguarded exception inside priceCart would surface as a raw stack trace with the wrong exit code.

P2 checks unknown_sku/invalid_quantity, then unknown_coupon, then unknown_state, then stock — the exact order the task text enumerates them — while P1 checks unknown_state and unknown_coupon (in priceCart) before the per-item unknown_sku/invalid_quantity loop, reordering relative to the spec's stated sequence.

P2's cartFail() can be thrown from anywhere and is caught once at the top, while P1's runCart repeats the same `if (result.error) { cartError(result.error); return; }` guard clause three times across parseInputFlag/readJson(input)/readJson(catalog)/priceCart, duplicating boilerplate that must be kept in sync as new steps are added.
